cards.front: What 4 pieces of IP information does a host need to function on a network?
cards.back: (1) IP address. (2) Subnet mask. (3) Default gateway IP. (4) DNS server IP(s). All can be learned via DHCP or set statically.

cards.front: What are the 4 messages of the DHCP DORA process?
cards.back: (D)iscover — client broadcasts looking for any DHCP server. (O)ffer — server unicasts/broadcasts an IP offer. (R)equest — client broadcasts which offer it accepts. (A)ck — server confirms the lease. After Ack the client can use the IP.

cards.front: Configure a Cisco IOS router as a DHCP server for the 10.0.20.0/24 subnet, excluding the gateway.
cards.back:
```
ip dhcp excluded-address 10.0.20.1 10.0.20.10
ip dhcp pool LAN20
 network 10.0.20.0 255.255.255.0
 default-router 10.0.20.1
 dns-server 8.8.8.8
 lease 0 12 0      ! 12 hours, default 1 day
```

cards.front: What is `ip helper-address` and where do you configure it?
cards.back: Configured on a router/L3-switch interface that receives DHCP broadcasts (the client-facing interface) — converts the broadcast to a unicast forwarded to the listed DHCP server. Example: `ip helper-address 10.1.1.5`. Without it, DHCP requests don't cross subnet boundaries.

cards.front: Configure a router interface as a DHCP CLIENT.
cards.back:
```
interface gi0/0
 ip address dhcp
 no shutdown
```
Useful for SOHO routers and lab scenarios where the router itself receives an IP from upstream (e.g., ISP modem).

cards.front: What command shows DHCP leases handed out by a Cisco DHCP server?
cards.back: `show ip dhcp binding` — lists IP-to-MAC mappings, lease expiration, type. `show ip dhcp pool` shows pool stats (utilization, range, leased count). `show ip dhcp conflict` shows duplicates.

cards.front: Default DHCP lease time on Cisco IOS, and how to change it?
cards.back: Default = 1 day. Configurable per pool with `lease <days> <hours> <minutes>` (e.g., `lease 7` = 7 days, `lease 0 12 0` = 12 hours, `lease infinite` = never expires).

cards.front: DNS record types: A, AAAA, CNAME, MX, NS, PTR, TXT — what does each map?
cards.back: A: hostname → IPv4 address. AAAA: hostname → IPv6 address. CNAME: alias to another hostname. MX: mail exchanger for a domain. NS: name server for a zone. PTR: reverse DNS (IP → hostname). TXT: free-form text (SPF, DKIM, verification).

cards.front: What L4 transport and port does DNS use, and when does it switch?
cards.back: Standard queries/responses use UDP/53. TCP/53 is used when responses exceed 512 bytes (large RRsets, DNSSEC) or for zone transfers (AXFR/IXFR between authoritative servers).

cards.front: Configure a Cisco router as a DNS resolver/server, and the related commands.
cards.back: `ip dns server` — turns the router itself into a tiny DNS responder. `ip name-server <ip>` — sets upstream resolvers the router queries. `ip domain lookup` (default on) — enables router DNS lookups. `ip domain name <name>` — sets default domain. `ip host <name> <ip>` — static host entry. View: `show hosts`.

cards.front: Useful Windows command-prompt IP / DNS / DHCP commands?
cards.back: `ipconfig` — basic IP info. `ipconfig /all` — detailed (MAC, DHCP, DNS). `ipconfig /release` — send DHCP Release. `ipconfig /renew` — send DHCP Discover. `ipconfig /displaydns` — show local DNS cache. `ipconfig /flushdns` — clear cache. `ping ip -n N` — N packets.

cards.front: Which DHCP DORA messages are broadcast vs. unicast, and which direction?
cards.back: Discover (client→server): always broadcast (client doesn't know server). Offer (server→client): can be either. Request (client→server): always broadcast (so other servers know which offer was chosen). Ack (server→client): can be either. Release (client→server): unicast. A DHCP relay agent (`ip helper-address`) converts broadcast Discover/Request to unicast toward the configured server.
